subtopics.title: Introduction to Voice – Active vs Passive

blocks.title: Active and Passive Voice
blocks.subtitle: Focusing on the Doer or the Action

blocks.title: What is Voice?
blocks.content: Voice is the form of a verb that indicates whether the subject performs the action (Active) or receives the action (Passive).

rows.right: Example: An apple is eaten by Ram.

blocks.title: Basic Conversion Steps
steps: Step 1: Identify the subject, verb, and object in the active sentence.
Step 2: Make the object the subject of the passive sentence.
Step 3: Use the appropriate form of 'be' + past participle (V3).
Step 4: Add 'by' before the original subject (if needed).
Step 5: Ensure the verb agrees with the new subject.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Only transitive verbs (verbs with objects) can be changed to passive.
Intransitive verbs (die, sleep, arrive) have no passive form.
The passive voice is formed with 'be' + V3.
'By' is used to introduce the doer of the action.

subtopics.title: Rules for Converting Active to Passive – Tense-wise Structure

blocks.title: Passive Structure Formula
blocks.formula:
Active: Subject + Verb + Object
Passive: Object + be (appropriate tense) + V3 + by + Subject
blocks.description: The verb 'be' changes according to the tense of the active sentence.

blocks.title: Tense-wise Passive Voice Structures
rows: A letter is written by her.
Obj + is/am/are + being + V3 + by + Sub
A letter is being written by her.
Obj + has/have + been + V3 + by + Sub
A letter has been written by her.
A letter was written by her.
Obj + was/were + being + V3 + by + Sub
A letter was being written by her.
Obj + had + been + V3 + by + Sub
A letter had been written by her.
Obj + will + be + V3 + by + Sub
A letter will be written by her.
Obj + will have + been + V3 + by + Sub
She will have written a letter.
A letter will have been written by her.

blocks.title: Trap: Passive of Present Perfect Continuous / Past Perfect Continuous.
blocks.wrong: The book is being written for 2 hours.
blocks.correct: The book has been written for 2 hours? Actually, continuous perfect tenses are rarely used in passive.
blocks.tip: Passive forms of Present Perfect Continuous and Past Perfect Continuous are generally avoided. Use Present Perfect or Past Perfect passive instead.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: The 'be' verb changes to match the tense of the active sentence.
The main verb is always in the past participle (V3) form.
The subject of the active sentence becomes the object of the preposition 'by'.

blocks.title: Example 1: Present Indefinite Passive
steps: Active: The teacher teaches English every day.
Object: English. Subject: The teacher.
Passive: English + is + taught + by the teacher + every day.
blocks.answer: English is taught by the teacher every day.
blocks.formula_used: Obj + is/am/are + V3 + by + Sub

blocks.title: Example 2: Present Continuous Passive
steps: Active: The chef is preparing the meal now.
Object: The meal. Subject: The chef.
Passive: The meal + is being + prepared + by the chef + now.
blocks.answer: The meal is being prepared by the chef now.
blocks.formula_used: Obj + is/am/are + being + V3 + by + Sub

blocks.title: Example 3: Present Perfect Passive
steps: Active: They have completed the project already.
Object: The project. Subject: They.
Passive: The project + has been + completed + by them + already.
blocks.answer: The project has been completed by them already.
blocks.formula_used: Obj + has/have + been + V3 + by + Sub

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Present Indefinite: is/am/are + V3.
Present Continuous: is/am/are + being + V3.
Present Perfect: has/have + been + V3.

blocks.title: Example 1: Past Indefinite Passive
steps: Active: The police arrested the thief.
Object: The thief. Subject: The police.
Passive: The thief + was + arrested + by the police.
blocks.answer: The thief was arrested by the police.
blocks.formula_used: Obj + was/were + V3 + by + Sub

blocks.title: Example 2: Past Continuous Passive
given: The workers (build) the bridge when the earthquake struck.
steps: Active: The workers were building the bridge when the earthquake struck.
Object: The bridge. Subject: The workers.
Passive: The bridge + was being + built + by the workers + when the earthquake struck.
blocks.answer: The bridge was being built by the workers when the earthquake struck.
blocks.formula_used: Obj + was/were + being + V3 + by + Sub

blocks.title: Example 3: Past Perfect Passive
given: The manager (finish) the report before the meeting.
steps: Active: The manager had finished the report before the meeting.
Object: The report. Subject: The manager.
Passive: The report + had been + finished + by the manager + before the meeting.
blocks.answer: The report had been finished by the manager before the meeting.
blocks.formula_used: Obj + had + been + V3 + by + Sub

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Past Indefinite: was/were + V3.
Past Continuous: was/were + being + V3.
Past Perfect: had + been + V3.

blocks.title: Example 1: Future Indefinite Passive
given: The company (launch) a new product next month.
steps: Active: The company will launch a new product next month.
Object: A new product. Subject: The company.
Passive: A new product + will be + launched + by the company + next month.
blocks.answer: A new product will be launched by the company next month.
blocks.formula_used: Obj + will be + V3 + by + Sub

blocks.title: Example 2: Future Perfect Passive
given: By next year, they (complete) the construction.
steps: Active: By next year, they will have completed the construction.
Object: The construction. Subject: They.
Passive: By next year, the construction + will have been + completed + by them.
blocks.answer: By next year, the construction will have been completed by them.
blocks.formula_used: Obj + will have been + V3 + by + Sub

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Future Indefinite: will be + V3.
Future Perfect: will have been + V3.
Future Continuous (will be + V1+ing) has NO passive form.

blocks.title: Passive with Modals
blocks.subtitle: Can, Could, May, Might, Must, Should, Would

blocks.title: Modal Passive Formula
blocks.formula:
Active: Subject + Modal + V1 + Object
Passive: Object + Modal + be + V3 + by + Subject
blocks.description: Modals are followed by 'be' + past participle in passive.

blocks.title: Example 1: Modal of Obligation
steps: Active: You must finish the work.
Object: The work. Subject: You.
Passive: The work + must + be + finished + by you.
blocks.answer: The work must be finished by you.
blocks.formula_used: Modal + be + V3

blocks.title: Example 2: Modal of Ability
steps: Active: She can solve this puzzle.
Object: This puzzle. Subject: She.
Passive: This puzzle + can + be + solved + by her.
blocks.answer: This puzzle can be solved by her.
blocks.formula_used: Modal + be + V3

blocks.title: Example 3: Modal Perfect Passive
steps: Active: They should have informed us.
Object: Us. Subject: They.
Passive: We + should have + been + informed + by them.
blocks.answer: We should have been informed by them.
blocks.formula_used: Modal + have + been + V3

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Modal + be + V3 for present/future passive.
Modal + have been + V3 for past passive.
Remember: 'be' is always used after the modal.

blocks.title: Rules for Imperatives
blocks.content: Imperative sentences (commands, requests) are converted to passive using 'Let' or 'You are requested/ordered to...'.

blocks.title: Example 1: Command (Let)
steps: Active: Open the door.
Passive structure: Let + object + be + V3.
Let the door be opened.
blocks.answer: Let the door be opened.
blocks.formula_used: Let + Obj + be + V3

blocks.title: Example 2: Request
steps: Active: Please help me.
Passive structure: You are requested + to + V1.
You are requested to help me.
blocks.answer: You are requested to help me.
blocks.formula_used: You are requested to + V1

blocks.title: Example 3: Negative Imperative
steps: Active: Do not waste water.
Passive structure: Let not + object + be + V3.
Let not water be wasted.
blocks.answer: Let not water be wasted.
blocks.formula_used: Let not + Obj + be + V3

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Commands → Let + object + be + V3.
Requests → You are requested to + V1.
Negative commands → Let not + object + be + V3.

blocks.title: Rules for Interrogatives
blocks.content: In passive, the helping verb comes before the new subject (object of active). The question word remains at the beginning.

blocks.title: Example 1: Yes/No Question
steps: Active: Did she write a letter?
Object: A letter. Subject: She.
Passive: Was a letter written by her?
blocks.answer: Was a letter written by her?
blocks.formula_used: Helping verb (be) + Object + V3 + by + Subject

blocks.title: Example 2: Wh-Question
steps: Active: Who wrote this book?
Object: This book. Subject: Who? (doer).
Passive: By whom was this book written?
blocks.answer: By whom was this book written?
blocks.formula_used: By whom + helping verb + object + V3

blocks.title: Example 3: Wh-Question with 'What'
steps: Active: What do you want?
Object: What. Subject: You.
Passive: What is wanted by you?
blocks.answer: What is wanted by you?
blocks.formula_used: What + helping verb + V3 + by + subject

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Yes/No Questions: Helping verb (be) + Object + V3 + by + Subject.
Wh-Questions: Wh-word + helping verb + Object + V3 + by + Subject.
For 'Who' questions, use 'By whom'.

subtopics.title: Passive with Two Objects (Double Object Verbs)

blocks.title: Passive with Two Objects
blocks.subtitle: Verbs like Give, Send, Tell, Show, Teach

blocks.title: What are Double Object Verbs?
blocks.content: Verbs that take both a direct and indirect object (e.g., give, send, tell, show, teach, promise). Examples: 'She gave me a book' (me = indirect, book = direct).

blocks.title: Example 1: Two Passive Options
given: The teacher gave the students a lesson.
blocks.find: Convert to passive in two ways.
steps: Active: The teacher gave the students a lesson.
Object 1 (Indirect): The students. Object 2 (Direct): A lesson.
Option A: The students were given a lesson by the teacher.
Option B: A lesson was given to the students by the teacher.
blocks.answer:
Option A: The students were given a lesson by the teacher.
Option B: A lesson was given to the students by the teacher.
blocks.formula_used: Either object can become the subject.

blocks.title: Example 2: Preference for Indirect Object
steps: Active: She showed me the photograph.
Indirect Object (me) is preferred as the subject.
Passive: I was shown the photograph by her.
blocks.answer: I was shown the photograph by her.
blocks.formula_used: Indirect object as subject (most common in exams)

blocks.title: Trap: Using wrong preposition with the retained object.
blocks.wrong: A lesson was given the students.
blocks.correct: A lesson was given to the students.
blocks.tip: When the direct object is the subject, the indirect object takes 'to' or 'for'.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Verbs like give, send, tell, show, teach, offer, promise take two objects.
Either object can become the subject.
When direct object becomes the subject, use 'to' or 'for' before the indirect object.

subtopics.title: Advanced Passive – With Prepositions, Bare Infinitives, and Infinitives

blocks.title: Advanced Passive Structures
blocks.subtitle: Prepositional Verbs, Bare Infinitives, and Infinitives

blocks.title: Passive with Prepositional Verbs
blocks.content: When the verb is followed by a preposition (e.g., look at, laugh at, talk about), the preposition is retained in the passive.

blocks.title: Example 1: Prepositional Verb
given: They laughed at the old man.
steps: Active: They laughed at the old man.
Object: The old man (preposition 'at' remains).
Passive: The old man was laughed at by them.
blocks.answer: The old man was laughed at by them.
blocks.formula_used: Object + was/were + V3 + preposition + by + Subject

blocks.title: Passive with Infinitives
blocks.content: Sentences with 'to + V1' (infinitive) become 'to be + V3' in passive.

blocks.title: Example 2: Passive with Infinitive
given: I want you to help me.
steps: Active: I want you to help me.
Object: me. Infinitive: to help.
Passive: I want to be helped by you.
blocks.answer: I want to be helped by you.
blocks.formula_used: I want + to be + V3 + by + Subject

blocks.title: Example 3: Bare Infinitive
given: We saw him cross the river.
steps: Active: We saw him cross the river.
After verbs of perception (see, hear, watch), the passive takes 'to + V1'.
Passive: He was seen to cross the river by us.
blocks.answer: He was seen to cross the river by us.
blocks.formula_used: Subject + was/were + seen/heard + to + V1 + by + agent

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Prepositions remain with the verb in passive.
Infinitives become 'to be + V3'.
Bare infinitives after perception verbs become 'to + V1' in passive.

blocks.title: Trap 1: Passive of Intransitive Verbs
blocks.correct: He died. (Intransitive verbs have no passive.)
blocks.tip: Verbs like die, sleep, arrive, happen, occur have no passive form.

blocks.title: Trap 2: Wrong form of 'be'
blocks.wrong: The book is wrote by him.
blocks.correct: The book is written by him.
blocks.tip: Always use the past participle (V3) form.

blocks.title: Trap 3: Omitting 'by' with agent
blocks.wrong: The letter was sent the manager.
blocks.correct: The letter was sent by the manager.
blocks.tip: Use 'by' before the doer in passive.

blocks.title: Trap 4: Passive of 'Let' in Negative
blocks.tip: Negative passive of imperative: Let not + object + be + V3.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Intransitive verbs have no passive.
Always use V3 (past participle) after 'be'.
Use 'by' to introduce the doer.
Check imperative and interrogative rules carefully.

blocks.title: Exam Strategy
blocks.content: Identify the tense of the active sentence. Apply the correct passive structure. Check subject-verb agreement in passive.

blocks.question: The book ______ by the teacher yesterday.
blocks.explanation: 'Yesterday' indicates past time. Passive of Past Indefinite: was/were + V3. 'Book' is singular → was given.

blocks.question: A new bridge ______ across the river.
blocks.explanation: The action is ongoing (present continuous passive) → is being built.

blocks.question: The thief ______ by the police last night.
blocks.explanation: 'Last night' indicates past time. Passive of Past Indefinite: was/were + V3. 'Thief' is singular → was arrested.

blocks.question: Please ______ the door.
blocks.explanation: Imperative passive: Let + object + be + V3 → Let the door be opened.

blocks.question: The work ______ by tomorrow.
blocks.explanation: 'By tomorrow' indicates future passive: will be + V3.

blocks.question: Why ______ the letter by him?
blocks.explanation: Passive of Past Indefinite question: Was + object + V3 + by + subject → Why was the letter posted by him?

blocks.question: The students ______ to study hard by the principal.
blocks.explanation: Passive of Past Indefinite: were + V3 → The students were advised to study hard by the principal.

blocks.question: She ______ a gift by her friend yesterday.
blocks.explanation: 'Yesterday' indicates past. Passive: She + was + given + by her friend.

blocks.question: Who ______ this letter?
blocks.explanation: Passive of 'Who wrote this letter?' → 'By whom was this letter written?' In options, 'Who was this letter written by?' is also acceptable. 'Who was written by' is the passive interrogative form.

blocks.question: The baby ______ by the mother carefully.
blocks.explanation: Present Continuous Passive: is being looked after.

blocks.title: Post-Test Review
blocks.content: Review the tense-wise structures. Practice double object and prepositional verb passives. Remember that intransitive verbs have no passive.

blocks.title: Final Revision – Active & Passive Voice
points: Active: Subject + Verb + Object.
Passive: Object + be (tense) + V3 + by + Subject.
Only transitive verbs have passive.
Imperatives: Let + object + be + V3.
Interrogatives: Helping verb + object + V3 + by + subject.
Double objects: Either can be subject (use 'to/for' for the other).
Prepositional verbs: Preposition remains.
Modal passive: Modal + be + V3.
Intransitive verbs: No passive.
